coredata.dc:title: Mn-CDs with oxidase activity produces ROS to inhibit cell activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiamei
Gao, Fucheng
Zhao, Li
Wu, Yanqiu
Wang, Fenglong
Dong, Lun
Jiang, Yanyan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) played increasingly important roles in biotechnology due to their potential as alternatives of natural enzymes. However, the fabrication of highly active and durable CDs remains challenging and catalytic reaction mechanisms over CDs needs to be better clarified for future advances. Herein, we synthesized manganese-doped carbon dots (Mn-CDs, ∼5 nm) with excellent oxidase properties which exhibits stable catalytic capacity in various salt solutions at different temperatures. Moreover, by thoroughly investigating catalytic ability of Mn-CDs, we found that doped Mn atoms effectively modulated the spin density and charge distribution of CDs, thus reducing the band gap by forming intermediate energy levels. Besides, it is found that the content of pyrrolic N positively correlated to catalytic capacity of Mn-CDs. As a demonstration, the prepared Mn-CDs could work as pseudo-oxidase in real cell environment. They can easily enter into cells, induce the production of ·O2 − under ultraviolet light irradiation, and eventually inhibit the activity of HeLa cells and 4T1 cells. We believe that the as-prepared Mn-CDs would find wide applications as metal-doped artificial nanozymes, and lay a foundation for the application and development of Mn-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozymes
Carbon dots
Mn doping
Oxidase-like activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetic assay of Ultraviolet-driven oxidase-like activity
Stability test of Mn-CDs as nanoenzymes
Subcellular localization and reactive oxygen species experiment of Mn-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of Mn-CDs. (b) TEM image of Mn-CDs (inset: size distribution histogram). (c) HRTEM image of Mn-CDs (inset: interplanar lattice spacing imaging). (d) XRD patterns of Mn-CDs and pure CDs. (e) EIS curves, (f) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (g) excitation and emission spectra of Mn-CDs and pure CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of pure CDs and Mn-CDs. (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s and (e) Mn 2p high resolution XPS spectra of Mn-CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of TMB oxidation by Mn-CDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB oxidation reaction under light irradiation of different wavelengths. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB oxidation reaction in ultraviolet light and darkness. (d) Comparison of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Mn-CDs-0.5 in different scavengers. Enzymatic stability of Mn-CDs-0.5 under different conditions: (e) pH values, (f) ionic strengths, (g) temperatures and (h) various metallic salt solutions. (i) The absorbance of TMB oxidation by Mn-CDs within 50 days of storage.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (a) Mn-CDs-0.5 and (b) pure CDs. Corresponding Tauc plot of (c) Mn-CDs-0.5 and (d) pure CDs. (e) CV curve of Mn-CDs-0.5 modified GC electrode in the nitrogen-saturated tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (0.1 M) with ferrocene as internal standard. (f) Band structure diagram of Mn-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) C 1s, (h) O 1s and (i) N 1s orbitals in Mn-CDs.

(a) Schematic intracellular catalytic mechanism of Mn-CDs under UV light excitation. (b) Cellular localization of Mn-CDs in HeLa cells. Effects of Mn-CDs on cell viability of (c) HeLa cells and (d) 4T1 cells. (e) Reactive oxygen species of different concentrations of Mn-CDs in HeLa cells. (*p < 0.05 and ***p < 0.0001).
Comparison of the apparent Michaelis-Menten constants (Km) and maximum reaction rates (Vmax) of different Mn-CDs.

head.ce:title: Mn-CDs with oxidase activity produces ROS to inhibit cell activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution & Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, and School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution & Processing of Materials
Ministry of Education, and School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution & Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, and School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Breast Surgery, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Breast Surgery, Qilu Hospital, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) played increasingly important roles in biotechnology due to their potential as alternatives of natural enzymes. However, the fabrication of highly active and durable CDs remains challenging and catalytic reaction mechanisms over CDs needs to be better clarified for future advances. Herein, we synthesized manganese-doped carbon dots (Mn-CDs, ∼5 nm) with excellent oxidase properties which exhibits stable catalytic capacity in various salt solutions at different temperatures. Moreover, by thoroughly investigating catalytic ability of Mn-CDs, we found that doped Mn atoms effectively modulated the spin density and charge distribution of CDs, thus reducing the band gap by forming intermediate energy levels. Besides, it is found that the content of pyrrolic N positively correlated to catalytic capacity of Mn-CDs. As a demonstration, the prepared Mn-CDs could work as pseudo-oxidase in real cell environment. They can easily enter into cells, induce the production of ·O2 − under ultraviolet light irradiation, and eventually inhibit the activity of HeLa cells and 4T1 cells. We believe that the as-prepared Mn-CDs would find wide applications as metal-doped artificial nanozymes, and lay a foundation for the application and development of Mn-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanoenzymes with intrinsic enzyme-like characteristics, have attracted increasing attention due to their potential as promising alternatives of natural enzymes [1–3]. A variety of nanomaterials have been proved to have enzyme-mimicking properties, such as Au [4], Ag [5], Pt [6], Fe3O4 [7], CeO2 [8], V2O5 [9] and graphene [10], etc. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) stand out due to their ultra-small size, unique optical and electronic properties and excellent biocompatibility [11]. The CDs-based artificial nanozymes have been reported to show peroxidase-like [12–14], oxidase-like [15,16], catalase-like [17] and superoxide-like [18] activities and their performance could be regulated by modulating the synthetic strategy [19–21], foreign element doping [22–24].

Heteroatom doping has been regarded as one of the most effective strategies to regulate and control the catalytic performance of CDs through optimizing their electronic properties and surface structure [18]. Since Feng et al. proposed in 2015 that the coordinated functionalization of charge exchange in carbon dots can be achieved by directly combining metal dopants with sp2-characterized heteroatoms, metal doped carbon dots have received increasing attention [19–25]. The previous studies also indicated that, the charge density of CDs could be modulated by metal ions as dopants and thus new intriguing properties would be expected [26–28]. For example, Yang et al. found that Fe, N-CDs had inherent peroxidase activity like that of natural iron porphyrin, and could catalyze TMB in the presence of H2O2 [29]. In another work, Wu et al. designed and synthesized Cu-CDs with photocatalytic ability, which showed excellent electron transfer performance in the photo-oxidation reaction of 1,4-DHP in air atmosphere [30]. Recently, Huang et al. preliminarily described the binding sites and formation mechanisms of transition-metal-atom-doped CDs, and found that Mn-CDs exhibited excellent catalytic performance to activate H2O2 and produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to degrade methylene blue and rhodamine B [25]. At present, the application value of CDs in catalysis have been successfully verified [26,27], whereas, the affecting factors governing the catalytic performance of CDs are less understood, which greatly hinder the further development of CDs-based nanoenzymes for biological applications and this highlights the necessity to prepare metal-doped CDs with oxidase-like properties and systematically unravel the factors affecting the catalytic performance of doped CDs.

Herein, we prepared Mn-CDs with an average size of 5 nm by hydrothermal method using EDTA-2Na as precursors. Benefiting from the synergistic contribution from Mn dopants and pyrrole N species, Mn-CDs exhibit promising oxidase activity for catalyzing TMB with dissolved oxygen. The superoxide radical (·O2 −) produced by Mn-CDs after oxygen activation greatly enhanced the oxidation of TMB into blue oxidation product ox-TMB under ultraviolet irradiation. Among the studied samples, Mn-CDs-0.5 showed the best catalytic activity, with the maximum reaction rate of 20.855 × 10−8 M/s. In addition, Mn-CDs can act as pseudo-oxidase in cells to inhibit the activity of Hela cells and 4T1 cells. This work on Mn-CDs for oxidase activity paves the avenue for the application of carbon dots in nanobiotechnology.

Manganerahydrate (MnC4H6O4·4H2O), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride hydrate (TMB), ferrocene, potassium ferricyanide, Potassium ferrocyanide trihydrate, sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), cobalt(II) chloride (CoCl2), iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), p-phthalic acid (TPA), superoxide dismutase (SOD), l-tryptophan (L-Try), anhydrous acetonitrile, tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate, Disodium terephthalate were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd.

Mn-CDs with different proportions was synthesized by hydrothermal method using EDTA-2Na as precursor. Briefly, manganerahydrate and EDTA-2Na were dissolved in 30 ml of ultrapure water at different molar ratios (0.25:1, 0.5:1, 1:1, 2:1, 0:1), then each of them was transferred to a 50 ml teflon lined stainless steel high-pressure hydrothermal tank and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the insoluble impurities in the solution were filtered by a 220 nm microporous filter, and dialyzed against a 1000 MW cellulose dialysis bag for 6 h to remove the unreacted precursor. Finally, five kinds of powders with different reactant ratios were obtained by freeze drying, which were entitled as Mn-CDs-0.25, Mn-CDs-0.5, Mn-CDs-1, Mn-CDs-2 and pure CDs, respectively.

3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was chosen as a substrate and the simulated oxidase activity of Mn-CDs was evaluated by the absorbance of TMB oxidation products at 652 nm. The reaction system contained: Mn-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) and TMB in water (1 mM). Subsequently, the reaction system was irradiated with ultraviolet light (365 nm) with an average intensity of 3W/cm2 and a radiation area of 3.14 cm2. Meanwhile, the reaction system was placed in a dark condition as a control group. The absorbance of the reaction system at 652 nm was measured by ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The experiment was carried out three times to ensure the accuracy of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic assay of Ultraviolet-driven oxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The kinetic process of Mn-CDs at pH 4.5 was measured by recording the absorbance of TMB oxidation product at 652 nm. The relationship between enzymatic reaction velocity and substrate concentration was fitted to the Michaelis-Menten equation, which was a classical model of enzymatic reaction kinetics. Michaelis-Menten equation (Eq. (1)) was shown as follow:
(1)
V =
[ S ]
×
V max
[ S ]
+
K m
Where Vmax was the maximum reaction velocity, reflecting the catalytic activity, and Km was the Michaelis constant, reflecting the affinity for substrates. Vmax and Km values could be plotted by Lineweaver Burk's double reciprocal plotting (Eq. (2)):
(2)
1 V
=
K m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max

By plotting the linear fit of the reciprocal of the reaction rate (1/V) against the reciprocal of the substrate concentration (1/[S]), the Vmax and Km values were calculated according to the slope andintercept.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability test of Mn-CDs as nanoenzymes
ce:section.ce:para: Mn-CDs solution (100 μg/mL), TMB solution (1 mM) were mixed under different conditions, including pH value (4–8), temperature (15°C–85 °C), ionic strength (0–1 M NaCl) and various 1 M metallic salt solutions (NaCl、MgCl2、KCl、CaCl2、MnCl2、CuCl2、NiCl2、CoCl2、FeCl2、FeCl3). After being irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light for 1 min, the absorbance of each reaction system at 652 nm was recorded by UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Subcellular localization and reactive oxygen species experiment of Mn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells (6 × 104 cells) were cultured in confocal dishes. After 24 h of incubation, the Control group and Mn-CDs group (0.1 mg/ml) were added to the culture dishes and incubated for 12 h. Then, the dishes were irradiated under 365 nm ultraviolet light for 5 min. After washing the cells with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) for three times, the treated cells were stained with DCFH-DA (Ex/Em = 488/525 nm) and DAPI (Ex/Em = 360/460 nm) according to the instructions. Then, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4), and the confocal images of the cells were recorded with a confocal microscope (FV1200, Olympus, Japan).

In addition, HeLa cells were also used to monitor the level of reactive oxygen species at different Mn-CDs concentrations and with or without ultraviolet irradiation. HeLa cells (6 × 104 cells) were cultured in confocal dishes. After 24 h of incubation, the Control group and Mn-CDs group (0.05 mg/ml, 0.1 mg/ml, 0.2 mg/ml, 0.4 mg/ml, 0.5 mg/ml) were added to the culture dishes and incubated for 2 h. Then, the dishes were irradiated under 365 nm ultraviolet light for 5 min. After washing the cells with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) for three times, the treated cells were stained with DCFH-DA (Ex/Em = 488/525 nm) and DAPI (Ex/Em = 360/460 nm) according to the instructions. Then, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4), and the confocal images of the cells were recorded with a confocal microscope (FV1200, Olympus, Japan).

HeLa cells and 4T1 cells were chosen as cell models to assess the cell viability of Mn-CDs. Briefly, HeLa cells and 4T1 cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were cultured in 96-well plates with DMEM medium and RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin solution, respectively. Then, the plates were transferred to a cell incubator filled with 5% of CO2 at 37 °C. After 24 h, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing different concentrations of Mn-CDs and cultured under 365 nm ultraviolet light and dark for 2 h. Then, cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was used to assess the cytotoxicity according to the absorbance at 450 nm.

Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (SPECORD 200 PLUS, Jena, Germany) was used to detect absorbance change and measure ultraviolet diffuse reflectance spectra. Transmission electron microscope (TEM; HT-7700, HITACHI) was used to characterize the morphology and size of Mn-CDs. The size distribution of Mn-CDs were observed by dynamic light scattering (DLS; ZETASIZER Nano series ZSE; Malvern). High power transmission electron microscope (FEI Talos F200X, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Czech Republic) was used to characterize the interplanar spacing of Mn-CDs. The fluorescence spectrometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to characterize the fluorescence spectra of Mn-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XRD; D8 DISCOVER, Japan Science Co., Ltd.) was used for phase analysis of Mn-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientifuc University) was used to analyze the functional group information of Mn-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Electron, USA) was used to characterize the elemental composition and structure of Mn-CDs. Electrochemical workstation (DH7000, Jiangsu donghua analysis instruments co., ltd.) was used to test electrochemical impedance and CV curves. Confocal microscope (FV1200, Olympus, Japan) was used to record confocal images of HeLa cells.

A series of Mn-CDs were fabricated using EDTA-2Na and MnC4H6O4·4H2O as starting materials through hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 10 h (Fig. 1 a). EDTA-2Na was chosen as the carbon precursor and chelating agent to combined Mn atom into the framework of CDs through groups such as OC and C–O during hydrothermal reaction. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image indicated that the well dispersed Mn-CDs with average diameter of 5 nm displayed typical spherical morphology (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Mn-CDs exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1c). Energy dispersive X-Ray elemental mappings confirmed the homogeneous distribution of C, N, O, and Mn over the Mn-CDs surface (Fig. S1). For the crystallographic structure, as shown in the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Mn-CDs and pure CDs, a broad peak at around 25°was observed, indicating the presence of carbon, and the absence of any peak of Mn-based species could be ascribed to the even dispersion and low content of Mn elements (Fig. 1d). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves (Fig. 1e) showed that Mn-CDs exhibited less resistance for electron transfer than pure CDs, indicating that Mn doping significantly enhanced the electron transport ability of CDs [31]. As displayed in Fig. 1f, the UV–Vis spectrum of Mn-CDs showed an obvious absorption band located at 200 nm, which could be ascribed to the π-π* transition of electron [28,29]. Meanwhile, Mn-CDs can emit intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. 1f). This phenomenon was ascribed to the relaxation of photoexcited electrons of π-π* transition to sp2 energy level [30]. Interestingly, in comparison with pure CDs, Mn-CDs exhibited red-shifted excitation and emission in wavelength region (Fig. 1g). Notably, the photoluminescent intensity of Mn-CDs was approximately fourteen times higher than that of pure CDs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the coordination between Mn atoms and functional groups on the surface of CDs [32]. It was reasonable to assume that the difference of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface led to the formation of intermediate energy level of π-π* transition in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the defect state, and the charge transfer between CDs led to the reduction of Mn-CDs band gap [33,34]. Meanwhile, as presented in Fig. S2d, the average PL-lifetime of pure CDs and Mn-CDs were 9.15 ns and 6.9 ns, respectively. The shorter lifetime for Mn-CDs was attributed to radiative recombination of carbon core excitons [35], and the shortened exciton localization results in faster radiative recombination, thus shortening the lifetime [36]. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yields of Mn-CDs and pure CDs are 3.63% and 1.21%, respectively (Fig. S4).

Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra of pure CDs and Mn-CDs were recorded to analyze their chemical functional groups, elemental compositions and surface chemical states. In the FTIR spectra of pure CDs and Mn-CDs (Fig. 2 a), the peaks at 1600 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, 1200 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–N in quinone ring, the asymmetric stretching vibration of CC, the stretching vibration of C–O and stretching vibration of CO, respectively [37]. Significantly, for the Mn-CDs, the new absorption peak at around 700 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of Mn–O bond. Hence, the FTIR analyses verified that Mn atoms were coordinated chemically within CDs.

The survey scan XPS spectrum of Mn-CDs exhibits four characteristic peaks at 285.42 eV, 399.41 eV, 531.05 eV and 641.01 eV, which arise from C, N, O and Mn elements, respectively (Fig. S5). The atomic ratios of each element in Mn-CDs were listed in Tab. S1, which is consistent with the ICP results in Tab. S2. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of Mn-CDs-0.5 could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 283.81 eV (C–C), 284.93 eV (CC) and 287.5 eV (CO) (Fig. 2b). Three peaks in the N 1s spectra were assigned to pyridine N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (399.28 eV) and graphitic N (400.98 eV) (Fig. 2c), illustrating that Mn-CDs were N-doped in nature and new C–N structures formed during the hydrothermal reaction. The peaks of the O 1s spectra at 530.87 eV and 534.87 eV corresponded to the C–O and CO groups (Fig. 2d), and the peaks of the Mn 2p spectra at 640.41 eV and 651.99 eV corresponded to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 (Fig. 2e). Among them, the resolved Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 peaks were assigned to Mn–O bond of MnO [36]. The C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Mn 2p high resolution XPS spectra of the remaining Mn-CDs were shown in Fig. S6. The above results showed that Mn-CDs with rich N-dopant have been successfully synthesized.

In order to evaluate the oxidase-like catalytic activity of the prepared CDs, oxidation of colorless TMB to blue oxidized product (oxTMB) was used as a probe reaction. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the bottles from left to right show the color changes of TMB oxidation by Mn-CDs-0.5, Mn-CDs-1, Mn-CDs-0.25, Mn-CDs-2 and pure CDs, respectively (the concentrations of all Mn-CDs were 1 mg/ml). By monitoring the characteristic absorption at 652 nm of oxTMB, it was found that Mn-CDs-0.5 had the best oxidation effect on TMB (Fig. S2c). In addition, under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, Mn-CDs-0.5 showed the best oxidation effect on TMB (Fig. 3b). Based on the fact that the colorimetric reaction of TMB can be carried out under ultraviolet irradiation but not in the dark (Fig. 3c), it is reasonable to deduce that Mn-CDs will show excellent catalytic performance when excited by ultraviolet light.

The effects of different scavengers on the activity of Mn-CDs enzyme were also investigated by active species capture experiments, in which EDTA, CuCl2, terephthalic acid (TPA), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and l-tryptophan (L-Try) were used as hole, electron, hydroxyl radical, superoxide radicals and singlet oxygen scavengers, respectively (Fig. 3d). The results showed that the absorption of each free radical scavenger decreased with the presence of Mn-CDs. Adding CuCl2 will reduce the oxidase-like activity of Mn-CDs, because the capture of electrons impedes the reaction. The addition of SOD will significantly inhibit the oxidase-like activity, indicating that superoxide radicals were the intermediate in the catalytic reaction.

The stability in complicated environment is a crucial parameter for assessing the durability of nanozymes. Like natural enzymes, the oxidase-like activity of Mn-CDs was also affected by pH values, temperature and ion concentration. Systematic investigations showed that the oxidase-like activity of Mn-CDs was highest at pH = 4 and showed a decreasing trend with the increase of pH value (Fig. 3e). Notably, the oxidase-like activity of Mn-CDs did not decrease significantly in a wide range of temperature (15 °C–85 °C) and ionic strength (0–1 M NaCl) (Fig. 3f and 3g). The ability of Mn-CDs to oxidize TMB remained in various metallic salt solutions except from Fe3+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ solutions (Fig. 3h). Moreover, the absorbance of Mn-CDs maintained after storage for 50 days, which further highlighted the stability of Mn-CDs (Fig. 3i). The above results showed that Mn-CDs as nanozyme was more tolerant to a wider range of temperature, ion concentration values and various metal salt solutions than natural enzymes, and it also showed that Mn-CDs have the potential as an alternative oxidase.

The electronic band positions were deduced by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests. Mn-CDs and pure CDs showed strong absorption across the ultraviolet and visible light region (Fig. 4 a and 4b), hinting their narrow band gap and high capacity in solar energy utilization for photo-induced chemical reactions [38]. Corresponding Tauc plots exhibited that Mn-CDs had similar optical band gaps, in which the optical band gaps of Mn-CDs-0.5 and pure CDs were 1.58 eV and 2.77 eV, respectively [2] (Fig. 4c and 4d). It was considered that the introduction of Mn atoms will effectively affect the spin density and charge distribution of CDs, thereby reducing the band gap by forming intermediate energy levels [2]. Taking the energy level of ferrocene as a reference, the positions of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Mn-CDs were calculated according to the initial values of oxidation and reduction potentials in CV curves (Fig. 4e and S8) [39,40]. In order to clarify whether Mn-CDs had the potential to reduce or oxidize other materials in thermodynamics, the electron volts were converted to the electrochemical potential (Tab. S4). As shown in Fig. 4f, the LUMO potentials of Mn-CDs were more negative than the energy level of oxygen to superoxide radical (
E
o 2
/ ·
O 2 −
= − 0.23 V
), indicating that Mn-CDs can reduce oxygen and generate superoxide radical [41].

Furthermore, the reaction kinetics over Mn-CDs as a simulated oxidase followed the typical Michaelise-Menten equation. The maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) were obtained from Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots, which were summarized in Table 1 . The Km value reveals the affinity of enzymes for substrates, and a lower Km value indicates greater substrate affinity. The catalytic activity is reflected by the Vmax value, which represents enzyme turnover [16]. Comparing the kinetic parameters over Mn-CDs with different Mn content, it was found that the Km values of Mn-CDs-0.25 (0.155 mM) and Mn-CDs-2 (0.155 mM) were significantly higher than that of Mn-CDs-0.5 (0.0978 mM). This indicated that doping of Mn atoms brings CDs with oxidase-like catalytic ability, but either too high or too low Mn content will result in weaker affinity between Mn-CDs and substrate. The Mn-CDs-0.5 gave the highest Vmax value, corresponding to the optimal activity among the studied materials. The above results showed that the catalytic ability of Mn-CDs depends on the doping of Mn.

The catalytic ability of Mn-CDs is also closely related to the functional groups in the Mn-CDs. The catalytic activity of CDs has also been demonstrated to be affected by their functional groups in previous studies [12]. FTIR and XPS were utilized to study the functional groups of these Mn-CDs. It was found that with the gradual increase of Mn content, the peak intensities of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups at 3500 cm−1,3200 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 gradually decreased, respectively. The relative contents of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements in different Mn-CDs were calculated from the peak areas of XPS results were listed in Tab. S5. It was found that the doping amount of Mn atoms did not affect the overall carbon content (Fig. 4g). However, the relative content of CO decreased gradually with the increase of Mn content (Fig. 4h). According to the “competitive inhibition” effect, the decreased affinity between Mn-CDs and substrate can be attributed to the markedly reduced CO groups. Furthermore, according to the integration of the N1s peak areas, we determined that the content of Pyrrolic N was in direct proportion to the catalytic capacity of Mn-CDs (Fig. 4i). We believed that the increased pyrrolic N content on the surface of Mn-CDs was beneficial to accelerate the electron flow between the carbon atoms and enhance their catalytic performance [2]. Thus, it could be concluded that the high catalytic ability of Mn-CDs could be ascribed to the Mn doping and pyrrolic N formation.

As mentioned above, Mn-CDs possessed oxidase-like activity through a superoxide radicals-mediated catalytic process under ultraviolet light irradiation. Besides, the extremely small Mn-CDs are expected to be taken in by cells, and induce the production of ROS under ultraviolet light (Fig. 5 a). To verify this postulation, a series of cytotoxicity and reactive oxygen species experiments were carried out with HeLa cells as model. Cell viability can be estimated by the oxidase-like activity of Mn-CDs in cells and the internalization of Mn-CDs in HeLa cells can be characterized by confocal microscope (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence signal of Mn-CDs (green) overlapped with that of mitochondrial dyes (deep red), indicating that Mn-CDs can be easily taken in by cells, which provided the chances for Mn-CDs to produce the ROS to inhibit cell activity.

As one of reactive oxygen radicals, superoxide radical can cause cell damage. Under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the viabilities of HeLa cells (250 μg/ml) and 4T1 cells (80 μg/ml) exposed to Mn-CDs were almost lower than those in the dark by 40% and 30%, respectively (Fig. 5c and 5d). This means that Mn-CDs can produce ROS under ultraviolet irradiation after entering cells, thus inhibiting the activity of HeLa and 4T1 cells.

In addition, in order to further verify that Mn-CDs can produce ROS in cells under the excitation of ultraviolet light, HeLa cells were used as model cells to set up different concentration groups for ROS experiments (Fig. 5e). As expected, with the increasing concentration of Mn-CDs, it was obvious that the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species gradually increased, which confirmed the biocatalytic activity of Mn-CDs in HeLa cells. All these results indicated that Mn-CDs can play a biocatalytic role as a simulated oxidase in real cell environment.

In summary, we successfully prepared a Mn-CDs based oxidase-like nanozyme with size of ∼5 nm, which can produce ROS in cells under the excitation of ultraviolet light, thus inhibiting cell activity. The as-prepared Mn-CDs exhibited satisfactory oxidase activity and had high tolerance in a wide range of temperatures and ionic solutions. It was confirmed that the catalytic ability of Mn-CDs depended dominantly on the doping of Mn atoms the pyrrole nitrogen content in Mn-CDs. In addition, cell experiments also verified that Mn-CDs nanozyme can be easily taken in by cells, and produce ROS under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light to inhibit the activity of HeLa cells and 4T1 cells. Overall, this work on catalytic performance of Mn-CDs was expected to provide guidance for the future research of doped carbon dots and nanozymes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 52101287 and 82002793]; the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province [Grant No. BK20190205]; and the Special Funding also supports this work in the Project of the Qilu Young Scholar Program of Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:para:
